mixed.question: What is the only fruit that has its seeds on the outside?
mixed.fact: A single strawberry has about 200 seeds on its outer surface!

mixed.question: Which country gifted the Statue of Liberty to the United States?
mixed.fact: France gifted it in 1886 to celebrate the friendship forged during the American Revolution.

mixed.question: In March 2026, which major sporting event is being prepared for its Summer 2026 debut in the USA, Canada, and Mexico?
mixed.fact: The 2026 FIFA World Cup will be the first ever hosted by three countries simultaneously!

mixed.question: How many time zones does Russia span?
mixed.fact: Russia spans 11 time zones — more than any other country on Earth!

mixed.question: Which famous composer went completely deaf but continued to write music?
mixed.fact: Beethoven composed his Ninth Symphony while entirely deaf — conducting its premiere himself.

mixed.question: What is the term for a group of flamingos?
mixed.fact: A group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance' — perfectly matching their fabulous pink style!

mixed.question: Which ocean is the Mariana Trench located in?
mixed.fact: The Mariana Trench is nearly 36,000 feet deep — deeper than Mount Everest is tall.

mixed.question: What letter does NOT appear in any U.S. state name?
mixed.fact: The letter Q is the only letter that doesn't appear in any of the 50 U.S. state names!

mixed.question: As of early 2026, NASA's James Webb Space Telescope has been most celebrated for imaging what?
options: A new planet in our solar system
The interior of a black hole
mixed.fact: JWST has captured light from galaxies formed just 300 million years after the Big Bang!

mixed.question: Which ancient writing system used wedge-shaped marks pressed into clay tablets?
mixed.fact: Cuneiform, invented by the Sumerians around 3400 BCE, is one of humanity's oldest writing systems.

science.question: What is the only mammal that can truly fly?
science.fact: Bats are the only mammals with powered flight — flying squirrels and colugos only glide!

science.question: Which planet has a day longer than its year?
science.fact: Venus rotates so slowly that one Venusian day (243 Earth days) outlasts its year (225 Earth days)!

science.question: What color is the blood of an octopus?
science.fact: Octopus blood uses copper-based hemocyanin instead of iron-based hemoglobin, making it blue!

science.question: Following the total solar eclipse visible from parts of Africa and Asia on March 29, 2025, when is the next total solar eclipse expected?
science.fact: The August 12, 2026 total solar eclipse will be visible from Greenland, Iceland, and Spain!

science.question: How many hearts does an earthworm have?
science.fact: Earthworms have five aortic arches that function like hearts, pumping blood through their bodies!

science.question: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
science.fact: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of Earth's atmosphere — oxygen is only around 21%!

science.question: Which of these animals can survive being frozen solid and then thawed?
science.fact: Wood frogs produce a natural antifreeze that protects their cells while up to 65% of their body freezes!

science.question: What is the Mpemba effect?
options: Cold water boils faster than warm water
Hot water can freeze faster than cold water
Ice melts faster in salt water
Steam condenses faster at high altitude
science.fact: Named after Tanzanian student Erasto Mpemba, this counterintuitive phenomenon still puzzles physicists today!

science.question: With NASA's Artemis program advancing in 2026, which mineral is a key resource scientists hope to extract from the Moon?
science.fact: Helium-3, rare on Earth but abundant on the Moon, could potentially fuel future nuclear fusion reactors!

science.question: What is a group of flamingos called?
science.fact: A group of flamingos is fittingly called a 'flamboyance' — matching their fabulous pink appearance!

history.question: Which empire was famously ruled by Suleiman the Magnificent during its golden age in the 16th century?
history.fact: Suleiman ruled for 46 years, making the Ottoman Empire a global superpower spanning three continents!

history.question: What year did the Great Fire of London devastate the city?
history.fact: The fire destroyed 13,200 houses but remarkably only six verified deaths were recorded!

history.question: Who was the Egyptian queen famous for her relationships with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony?
history.fact: Cleopatra spoke at least nine languages and was the first Ptolemaic ruler to learn Egyptian!

history.question: March 2026 marks the 190th anniversary of which famous 1836 battle in Texas?
history.fact: The Battle of the Alamo lasted 13 days and became a rallying cry for Texas independence!

history.question: Which medieval document, signed in 1215, limited the power of the English monarchy?
history.fact: Only four original copies of the 1215 Magna Carta survive today!

history.question: The Terracotta Army was buried with which Chinese emperor to protect him in the afterlife?
history.fact: Over 8,000 unique soldier statues were discovered — no two faces are exactly alike!

history.question: Which country was the first to grant women the right to vote in national elections?
history.fact: New Zealand granted women suffrage in 1893, nearly 30 years before the United States did!

history.question: What was the code name for the Allied invasion of Normandy on June 6, 1944?
history.fact: Over 156,000 troops crossed the English Channel on D-Day, the largest amphibious invasion ever!

history.question: The ancient Library of Alexandria, one of history's greatest repositories of knowledge, was located in which modern country?
history.fact: The library may have held up to 400,000 scrolls, representing virtually all ancient world knowledge!

history.question: With the 250th anniversary of American independence approaching in 2026, which country was the first to officially recognize the United States?
history.fact: Morocco recognized the U.S. in 1777, and their treaty of 1786 is America's longest unbroken alliance!

popculture.question: Which iconic TV show featured a character named 'The Fonz' who could fix a jukebox by hitting it?
popculture.fact: Henry Winkler's audition for Fonzie was so cool, producers rewrote the character to be a lead!

popculture.question: What is the name of the fictional country where Marvel's Black Panther is king?
popculture.fact: Wakanda first appeared in Fantastic Four #52 in 1966, before Black Panther got his own series.

popculture.question: Which pop star's 2025-2026 world tour broke Taylor Swift's Eras Tour record for single-tour gross revenue?
options: No artist has broken it yet
popculture.fact: Taylor Swift's Eras Tour grossed over $2 billion, a record that still stands as of early 2026!

popculture.question: In the movie 'The Wizard of Oz,' what color are Dorothy's iconic slippers?
popculture.fact: In L. Frank Baum's original book, the slippers were actually silver, not ruby red!

popculture.question: Which streaming platform released its first original film, 'Beasts of No Nation,' in 2015?
popculture.fact: Netflix now produces more original content annually than all major Hollywood studios combined.

popculture.question: The 2026 Academy Awards ceremony in March marks which milestone year for the Oscars?
popculture.fact: The 98th Oscars in 2026 continue a tradition that started with just 270 guests at a banquet in 1929!

popculture.question: Which band holds the record for the best-selling album of all time with 'Thriller'?
popculture.fact: Thriller has sold an estimated 70 million copies worldwide — roughly one for every 114 people on Earth!

popculture.question: In the anime 'Dragon Ball Z,' what is the name of the technique where two characters fuse by performing a synchronized dance?
popculture.fact: The Fusion Dance must be perfectly symmetrical — even a slight error creates a weak, deformed fusion!

popculture.question: Which legendary video game franchise features locations called Hyrule, Skyloft, and the Great Sea?
popculture.fact: Zelda creator Shigeru Miyamoto was inspired by exploring caves and forests near his childhood home.

popculture.question: Which reality TV competition has the most Emmy Award wins of any reality show in history?
popculture.fact: The Amazing Race won Outstanding Reality-Competition Program for 10 consecutive years from 2003 to 2012!

geography.question: What is the only country in the world that spans all four hemispheres (Northern, Southern, Eastern, and Western)?
geography.fact: Kiribati stretches across the equator and the International Date Line, touching all four hemispheres!

geography.question: Which African country has the most pyramids — even more than Egypt?
geography.fact: Sudan has over 200 pyramids, nearly double Egypt's count, mostly built by the Kingdom of Kush!

geography.question: What is the longest river in Europe?
geography.fact: The Volga stretches about 3,530 km through Russia, dwarfing every other European river!

geography.question: Which country has the most natural lakes in the world?
geography.fact: Canada holds over 60% of the world's lakes — more than all other countries combined!

geography.question: As Indonesia prepares for its massive capital city relocation in 2026, what is the name of the new capital replacing Jakarta?
geography.fact: Nusantara is being built on Borneo island because Jakarta is literally sinking into the sea!

geography.question: Which capital city sits at the highest elevation above sea level?
geography.fact: La Paz sits at roughly 3,640 meters — so high that visitors often get altitude sickness!

geography.question: On which continent would you find the Dry Valleys, one of the driest places on Earth?
geography.fact: Antarctica's McMurdo Dry Valleys haven't seen rain for roughly 2 million years!

geography.question: Which strait separates Europe from Africa at their closest point?
geography.fact: The Strait of Gibraltar is only about 14 km wide — you can see Africa from Spain!

geography.question: With Greenland pursuing greater autonomy from Denmark in 2025-2026, approximately what percentage of Greenland is covered by ice?
geography.fact: Greenland's ice sheet is so massive that if it fully melted, sea levels would rise about 7 meters!

geography.question: Which landlocked country is often called the 'Roof of the World'?
geography.fact: Tajikistan's Pamir Mountains average over 4,000 meters and were an ancient Silk Road crossroads!

sports.question: Which NBA player holds the record for the most points scored in a single game?
sports.fact: Wilt Chamberlain scored 100 points for the Philadelphia Warriors on March 2, 1962!

sports.question: In soccer, how many players from one team are on the field during a standard match?
sports.fact: Eleven players per side has been the rule since 1897 when FIFA standardized it.

sports.question: Which country has won the most FIFA World Cup titles as of 2026?
sports.fact: Brazil has won the World Cup five times — 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002!

sports.question: With the 2026 FIFA World Cup expanding this summer, how many teams will compete for the first time in the tournament?
sports.fact: The 2026 World Cup in the US, Canada, and Mexico will be the first with 48 teams!

sports.question: Which sport uses the term 'triple axel'?
sports.fact: A triple axel involves 3.5 rotations in the air — the hardest common jump in skating.

sports.question: Who holds the record for the most Grand Slam tennis singles titles in men's history?
sports.fact: Novak Djokovic surpassed Nadal's 22 Grand Slams, reaching 24 by early 2024!

sports.question: In baseball, what is the distance from the pitcher's mound to home plate?
sports.fact: The quirky 60'6" distance reportedly resulted from a surveyor misreading '60-0' as '60-6'!

sports.question: As March Madness 2026 tips off this week, how many teams compete in the NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament?
sports.fact: The tournament expanded to 68 teams in 2011 with the addition of the 'First Four' games.

sports.question: Which Olympic sport involves stones, a house, and sweeping?
sports.fact: Curling originated in medieval Scotland, with the oldest curling stone dating to 1511!

sports.question: In cricket, what is a score of zero by a batsman commonly called?
sports.fact: The term 'duck' comes from 'duck's egg,' referring to the oval zero shape on scoreboards.

food.question: What spice is made from the dried stigmas of a flower and is often called the most expensive spice in the world?
food.fact: It takes about 75,000 saffron flowers to produce just one pound of the spice!

food.question: Which fruit is known as a 'berry' botanically, even though most people wouldn't guess it?
food.fact: Bananas are technically berries, while strawberries and raspberries are not — botany is wild!

food.question: What common kitchen ingredient has a virtually unlimited shelf life if stored properly?
food.fact: Archaeologists found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still perfectly edible!

food.question: In March 2026, Japan's cherry blossom season is kicking off — which traditional Japanese drink is often released in a sakura-flavored version each spring?
food.fact: Major sake breweries release limited sakura editions every spring, infused with cherry blossom petals.

food.question: Which country is the world's largest producer of olive oil?
food.fact: Spain produces nearly half of the world's olive oil, with vast groves covering Andalusia.

food.question: What is the main ingredient in the traditional Middle Eastern dip baba ganoush?
food.fact: Baba ganoush gets its smoky flavor from charring the eggplant over an open flame.

food.question: With the 2026 FIFA World Cup approaching, which host country — Mexico — is famous for which ancient fermented drink made from agave?
food.fact: Pulque has been brewed from agave sap for over 1,000 years and was sacred to the Aztecs.

food.question: Which cheese is traditionally stored in caves and contains veins of blue-green mold?
food.fact: Roquefort is aged in the natural limestone caves of Combalou in southern France.

food.question: What is the Scoville Heat Unit (SHU) range of a common jalapeño pepper?
food.fact: The Carolina Reaper can reach over 2 million SHU — about 300 times hotter than a jalapeño!

food.question: Which Asian condiment is made by fermenting soybeans, wheat, salt, and water for months or even years?
food.fact: Traditional soy sauce can be fermented for up to three years, deepening its complex umami flavor.

music.question: What instrument does a person play if they are called a 'timpanist'?
music.fact: Timpani are tuned drums that can produce specific pitches, making them unique among percussion instruments!

music.question: Which legendary musician was known as the 'King of Pop'?
music.fact: Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' remains the best-selling album of all time with over 70 million copies sold.

music.question: How many strings does a standard ukulele have?
music.fact: The ukulele's name roughly translates to 'jumping flea' in Hawaiian — inspired by the finger movements!

music.question: Which composer wrote the famous 'Moonlight Sonata' in 1801 while gradually losing his hearing?
music.fact: Beethoven was nearly completely deaf by the time he composed his Ninth Symphony — he never heard it performed.

music.question: In music theory, how many semitones make up a perfect fifth interval?
music.fact: The perfect fifth is considered the most consonant interval after the octave and is the basis of power chords!

music.question: Which country is the birthplace of reggae music?
music.fact: Reggae evolved from ska and rocksteady in the late 1960s and was popularized worldwide by Bob Marley.

music.question: Celebrating its 50th anniversary in March 2026, which iconic band released 'Destroyer' in 1976?
music.fact: KISS's 'Destroyer' featured the hit 'Beth,' a ballad that became one of their biggest singles ever!

music.question: What musical term indicates a piece should be played gradually getting louder?
music.fact: Crescendo comes from the Italian word 'crescere' meaning 'to grow' — music is full of Italian terms!

music.question: Which avant-garde composer wrote '4'33",' a piece consisting entirely of silence?
music.fact: John Cage intended the 'music' to be the ambient sounds the audience hears during the silence!

music.question: What is the term for a group of five musicians performing together?
music.fact: The most famous quintet form is the string quintet, which typically adds a second viola or cello.

technology.question: What does the acronym 'USB' stand for?
technology.fact: USB was released in 1996 and was co-invented by Ajay Bhatt at Intel!

technology.question: How many bits are in a single byte?
technology.fact: A byte can represent 256 different values, from 0 to 255!

technology.question: Which company created the first commercially successful graphical user interface (GUI)?
technology.fact: Apple's Macintosh (1984) popularized the GUI, though Xerox PARC invented the concept earlier!

technology.question: In March 2026, NVIDIA's latest Blackwell Ultra GPUs are dominating AI training. What was NVIDIA originally best known for?
technology.fact: NVIDIA's 1999 GeForce 256 was marketed as the world's first GPU — now they power most AI!

technology.question: What programming language shares its name with an island in Indonesia?
technology.fact: Java was named after Java coffee from the island — the logo is literally a coffee cup!

technology.question: What does 'HTTP' stand for in a web address?
technology.fact: HTTP was invented by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 at CERN alongside the World Wide Web!

technology.question: Which data structure operates on a 'Last In, First Out' (LIFO) principle?
technology.fact: Think of a stack of plates — you always take from the top, just like a data stack!

technology.question: As of early 2026, which AI model architecture remains the backbone of most large language models?
technology.fact: The Transformer was introduced in Google's legendary 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need'!

technology.question: What was the original name of the Windows operating system's built-in web browser that debuted in 1995?
technology.fact: Internet Explorer once held 95% market share but was officially retired in June 2022!

technology.question: In computing, what does a 'qubit' represent?
options: The basic unit of quantum information
technology.fact: Unlike classical bits (0 or 1), qubits can exist in superposition — being both simultaneously!

nature.question: What is the only mammal capable of true sustained flight?
nature.fact: Bats are the only mammals with wings that allow true powered flight, not just gliding!

nature.question: How many hearts does an octopus have?
nature.fact: Octopuses have three hearts — two pump blood to the gills and one pumps it to the body!

nature.question: Which bird is known for being able to fly backward?
nature.fact: Hummingbirds can fly backward, upside down, and hover in place thanks to their unique wing rotation!

nature.question: As spring 2026 approaches in the Northern Hemisphere, which animal hormone triggers the onset of migration in many bird species?
nature.fact: Corticosterone spikes help trigger migratory restlessness, called 'zugunruhe,' as daylight increases in spring!

nature.question: What is the largest species of penguin currently alive?
nature.fact: Emperor penguins can stand nearly 4 feet tall and survive Antarctic winters at -60°C!

nature.question: Which sea creature has the largest eyes in the animal kingdom?
nature.fact: Colossal squid eyes can be up to 27 cm across — the size of a dinner plate!

nature.question: What unique adaptation allows the wood frog to survive being frozen solid during winter?
options: Glucose flooding its cells as cryoprotectant
nature.fact: Wood frogs flood their cells with glucose, acting like antifreeze to prevent ice crystal damage!

nature.question: With the UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration underway, which biome stores the most carbon per unit area on Earth?
nature.fact: Peatlands cover just 3% of Earth's land but store roughly twice as much carbon as all forests combined!

nature.question: What is a group of flamingos called?
nature.fact: A group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance' — perfectly matching their fabulous pink appearance!

nature.question: Which plant is known for producing the largest flower in the world?
nature.fact: Rafflesia arnoldii blooms can reach over 3 feet wide and smell like rotting flesh to attract pollinators!

film.question: Which 1994 film was famously beaten by 'Forrest Gump' for Best Picture at the Oscars?
film.fact: The Shawshank Redemption received 7 Oscar nominations but won zero — yet became the top-rated film on IMDb!

film.question: What is the name of the fictional country where 'Black Panther' takes place?
film.fact: Wakanda first appeared in Marvel comics in 1966, created by Stan Lee and Jack Kirby!

film.question: Who directed the 2010 sci-fi film 'Inception'?
film.fact: Nolan spent 10 years writing the Inception screenplay before he felt ready to film it!

film.question: In the TV series 'Breaking Bad,' what is Walter White's street alias?
film.fact: The alias references real physicist Werner Heisenberg, famous for the uncertainty principle!

film.question: Which actress holds the record for the most Academy Award wins for Best Actress?
film.fact: Katharine Hepburn won 4 Best Actress Oscars and never attended any of the ceremonies to collect them!

film.question: With the 2026 Oscars ceremony recently held, which studio's animated film 'Flow' won Best Animated Feature with no dialogue?
film.fact: Flow was Latvia's first Oscar-winning film and was made on a tiny budget of around $3.7 million!

film.question: In 'The Wizard of Oz' (1939), what color are Dorothy's famous slippers?
film.fact: In L. Frank Baum's original book, the slippers were silver — they were changed to ruby for Technicolor!

film.question: Which TV show holds the record for the most Emmy Awards won by a comedy series?
film.fact: Frasier won 37 Primetime Emmys over its run, including five consecutive Outstanding Comedy Series wins!

film.question: What was the first fully computer-animated feature film ever released?
film.fact: Toy Story (1995) was made with 800,000 machine hours of rendering — each frame took 4-13 hours!

film.question: Which legendary director made his debut at age 24 with the TV movie 'Duel' (1971) about a menacing truck driver?
film.fact: Spielberg filmed Duel in just 16 days on a shoestring budget, and it was so good it got a theatrical release!

politics.question: Which country has the world's longest-serving current head of state as of March 2026?
politics.fact: Teodoro Obiang has ruled Equatorial Guinea since 1979, making him the longest-serving non-royal leader alive!

politics.question: What is the official term for a country with no coastline?
politics.fact: There are 44 landlocked countries in the world, with Liechtenstein being doubly landlocked!

politics.question: Which international organization has its headquarters in The Hague, Netherlands, and prosecutes war crimes?
politics.fact: The ICC was established in 2002 and has jurisdiction over genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity.

politics.question: In March 2026, which country holds the rotating presidency of the Council of the European Union?
politics.fact: Denmark took over the EU Council presidency in January 2026, running through June 2026!

politics.question: How many permanent members sit on the United Nations Security Council?
politics.fact: The five permanent members — US, UK, France, Russia, and China — each hold veto power.

politics.question: Which treaty, signed in 1648, is often credited with establishing the modern concept of national sovereignty?
politics.fact: The Peace of Westphalia ended the Thirty Years' War and created the idea of sovereign nation-states!

politics.question: What does the political term 'gerrymandering' refer to?
politics.fact: The term comes from Governor Elbridge Gerry, whose 1812 redistricting map resembled a salamander!

politics.question: Which African nation was never colonized by a European power?
politics.fact: Ethiopia successfully resisted Italian invasion at the Battle of Adwa in 1896, preserving its independence.

politics.question: As tensions continue in the South China Sea in early 2026, how many countries border this disputed body of water?
politics.fact: China, Vietnam, Philippines, Malaysia, Brunei, and Taiwan all have overlapping territorial claims there!

politics.question: Which political philosophy, associated with thinkers like John Rawls, emphasizes justice as fairness and individual rights?
politics.fact: Rawls' 1971 book 'A Theory of Justice' revolutionized political philosophy with his famous 'veil of ignorance.'
